results.title: What Are the Most Common Complaints About AI Customer Support ...
results.content:
## Complaint 3: The AI Does Not Understand Context

"I already explained this." Few phrases in customer support carry as much frustration as this one. When AI tools lack context awareness, customers are forced to repeat their issue with every interaction, re-explain their order details, and start from scratch if they switch channels.

Why it happens: AI tools that operate as isolated chatbots without integration into your CRM, ticketing system, or order management platform cannot access customer history. They treat every interaction as a brand new conversation. [...] Key takeaways:

 Hallucinated or inaccurate answers are the number one complaint driving customer frustration
 Poor escalation to human agents traps customers in unhelpful AI loops
 Lack of context forces customers to repeat themselves across interactions
 Rigid, scripted responses fail to address nuanced customer issues
 Twig addresses these complaints through source-attributed accuracy and smart integration design

## Complaint 1: The AI Gives Wrong or Made-Up Answers

This is the most damaging complaint and the most common. AI hallucination, where the model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information, is the fundamental trust-breaker in customer support. [...] How to avoid it: Choose AI platforms that integrate deeply with your existing support stack. When your AI can pull customer history from Zendesk, order data from your commerce platform, and previous conversation context from your CRM, it can provide personalized, context-aware responses.

## Complaint 4: Responses Feel Robotic and Scripted

Customers can tell when they are talking to a poorly implemented AI. Overly formal language, generic responses that do not address the specific question, and rigid conversation flows all create an experience that feels more frustrating than helpful.

results.title: Consumer complaints to FTC on AI tools: Deceptive practices, poor service, sexual content | FedScoop
results.content:
A Federal Trade Commission database meant to guide law enforcement investigations now includes about 200 complaints against major artificial intelligence companies including xAI, OpenAI, and Anthropic, according to documents obtained through a public records request.

Those complaints include allegations of deceptive business practices and shoddy customer service, but also more severe accusations, including claims that these AI systems have joked about sexually assaulting children and spewed antisemitic rhetoric.

The claims, which were anonymized by public records officials, haven’t been verified by FedScoop, but provide insight into some of the frustrations that people are bringing to both the government and consumer advocacy groups like the Better Business Bureau. [...] FedScoop isn’t able to verify the complaints, and it’s possible that these problems weren’t truly caused by the company, or that the allegations are unfounded. Still, the database provides a view into the varied kinds of frustrations people have with AI that are then brought to government investigators.

Anthropic and OpenAI declined to comment. xAI did not respond to a request for comment.

It includes accusations of throttled capacity and absent customer support agents. Some complaints outline more haunting experiences with AI, while others complain about crypto services they’ve confused for the Elon Musk-led firm xAI.

Here are unedited excerpts of a few of the complaints. The rest are available in full form here.

results.title: AI Told Me to Start This Business. So I Did.
results.content:
Steps:
1. Scrape reviews across multiple platforms (Capterra, G2, GetApp, TrustRadius, Reddit, app store reviews if available).
2. Collect at least 200+ recent reviews (past 2 years).
3. Parse and cluster feedback into two categories:
   - Pain points (things users hate, churn reasons, frustrations, broken features, cost complaints).
   - Must-haves (the specific features or outcomes users rave about and would never give up).
4. For each cluster, include:
   - Direct sample quotes from real users (keep them short).
   - Frequency (# of times it appeared across reviews).
   - Sentiment strength (light annoyance → dealbreaker).
5. Summarize into a ranked list:
   - Top 10 pain points across both platforms.
   - Top 10 must-have features across both platforms. [...] four minutes. It's so easy and it costs about $2 a year the way I do it. Okay. The sentiment 
for QuickBooks is 38 out of 100 compared to 48 out of 100 for zero. Significant number of users 
are expressing frustration over pricing complexity and technical issues. Okay, so basically people 
generally dislike both QuickBooks and Zero, but they like Zero a little better. Now 
we can go to the ask feature and ask AI a question about Zero or QuickBooks or whatever 
features people hate or can't live without, and it will answer us based on all of these posts 
in all of these subreddits. What complaint occurs the most often regarding both QuickBooks and 
Zero? That is the question I'm asking AI. Okay, the number one complaint about QuickBooks and [...] themes. The number one pain point, frequent bugs, crashes, and unreliable updates. Number two, price 
hikes and unpredictable subscription costs. I find this very interesting. It's not just that it's 
expensive, it's that it's unpredictably expensive and the price just keeps going up. Poor customer 
support, limited report customization, payroll limitations, yada yada yada. But usually when 
you're looking at lists of complaints like this, the 8020 rule is very much in force. As in 80% of 
people are going to have these top two complaints and 20% of people are going to have the bottom 
eight complaints. The must-have features that Chat GPT5 brought back are cloud accessibility and 
multi-user collaboration, sharing your account, adding team members with your accountant, your

## FOX 2 St. Louis
289000 subscribers
7 likes

### Description
655 views
Posted: 25 Jun 2026
Artificial intelligence seems here to stay, but it’s not without some pitfalls. In the last three years, the Better Business Bureau has received more than 100,000 complaints on AI. More than 90% of those complaints mention a negative experience with AI services. Sarah Wetzel, a BBB spokeswoman, visits FOX 2 News at Midday to discuss these growing concerns.
